title: NIS2 Backup Management and Disaster Recovery Verification
exclusions: Does not perform the physical restore itself, certify vendor support contracts, or waive legal duties when backups fail — it only surfaces facts for humans to act on.
evidence: Hourly alert `UC-22.2.17`, weekly compliance workbook, and RFC3161 countersignature bundle stored beside the DR test calendar.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(c) requires backup management and disaster recovery. The UC verifies backup job completion, retention compliance, and RPO adherence from backup infrastructure telemetry in Splunk. It cannot confirm that restores actually produce usable data or that the BCP was exercised, hence partial.
compliance.controlObjective: Merges `backup:job_status` runs and `backup:restore_test` outcomes with `nis2_backup_schedule` so failed jobs, missed RPO windows, and failing drills share one timeline.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: `UC-22.2.17` saved search on `index=backup` (`sourcetype=backup:job_status`, `sourcetype=backup:restore_test`) with `lookup nis2_backup_schedule` (system_name, backup_policy, rpo_hours, rto_hours); exports vaulted under RFC3161. SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a failed `backup:job_status` row for `host=SAP-PRD-01` with `status=Failed` and confirm the hourly alert fires; set `rpo_hours=4` in the lookup while leaving `last_evt` older than four hours for the same `system_key` and verify `rpo_breach=1`; ingest `backup:restore_test` with `test_result=failed` and confirm `any_restore_fail` stays set until a passing row arrives.
controlTest.negativeScenario: After bringing all jobs to `success` inside the RPO window and logging a passing restore test within SLA, run the search across the last seven days and expect an empty result set while the backup console still shows green history — proving tuning does not mask fresh failures because the alert window is sliding hourly.

dataSources: `index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status` via Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601) or generic HEC: `job_name`, `status`, `host`, `bytes_transferred`, `duration_sec`. `index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test` (Commvault, NetBackup, Veeam SureBackup style exports): `test_id`, `test_result`, `target_system`, `completed_at`. Lookup `nis2_backup_schedule.csv` binds `system_name` to `backup_policy`, `rpo_hours`, `rto_hours`, `last_successful_backup`. Optional `index=itsi_summary` for drill overlays (Splunkbase 1841).
app: Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601), optional Splunk IT Service Intelligence (Splunkbase 1841); define HEC token `backup-hec` on indexers if sending raw JSON
spl:
(index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status earliest=-14d@d) OR (index=backup sourcetype=backup:restore_test earliest=-120d@d)
| eval system_key=coalesce(host,target_system,vm_name,client_name)
| eval lane=if(sourcetype="backup:job_status","backup_job","restore_test")
| lookup nis2_backup_schedule system_name AS system_key OUTPUT backup_policy, rpo_hours, rto_hours, last_successful_backup
| eval job_status=lower(coalesce(status,job_result,test_result,state))
| eval job_failed=if(lane="backup_job" AND match(job_status,"fail|error|warning|cancel"),1,0)
| eval restore_failed=if(lane="restore_test" AND NOT match(job_status,"pass|success|ok"),1,0)
| eval last_evt=_time
| stats latest(last_evt) as last_evt max(job_failed) as any_job_fail max(restore_failed) as any_restore_fail by system_key lane
| eval hours_since=round((now()-last_evt)/3600,1)
| eval rpo_breach=if(lane="backup_job" AND isnotnull(rpo_hours) AND hours_since>rpo_hours,1,0)
| eval combined_risk=if((any_job_fail>0) OR (any_restore_fail>0) OR (rpo_breach>0),1,0)
| where combined_risk=1
| table system_key lane hours_since rpo_hours any_job_fail any_restore_fail backup_policy
| sort 0 - hours_since
description: Tracks backup engine run outcomes plus restore-test records against each system row’s RPO/RTO targets, calling out missed cadence, failed copy jobs, and restore checks that never reached success.
value: Restore confidence improves because operations and compliance share one timeline that states when data was last protected, when it was last proven recoverable, and which machines still breach the agreed window.
implementation: (1) Install Splunk Add-on for Veeam (Splunkbase 5601) or send `backup:job_status` over HEC with extractions in `props.conf`. (2) Ingest restore-test reports as `backup:restore_test`. (3) Populate `nis2_backup_schedule.csv`. (4) Alert when `combined_risk=1`. (5) Archive weekly PDF with RFC3161 signing.
visualization: Row-level table for breach reasons; timeline panel of `last_evt`; single value widgets for count of systems past `rpo_hours`; optional ITSI Glass Table linked by `system_key`.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601)

knownFalsePositives:
**Weekly synthetic fulls that always warn:** Distinguish vendor warning codes that still exit zero. Suppress with `where NOT match(job_status,".*catalog.*warning")` after validating with backup admins.

**Restore tests on disposable clones:** Distinguish `target_system` prefixed `LAB-`. Suppress using `lookup nis2_backup_schedule system_name AS system_key OUTPUT environment | where environment!="lab"`.

**Snapshot-only workloads without legacy agent jobs:** Distinguish storage-array copies not emitting `backup:job_status`. Use a time-bound exception register entry until HEC coverage ships.

**Long RPO business acceptance:** Distinguish systems with board-approved 168h RPO. Suppress by updating `rpo_hours=168` in the CSV instead of muting the alert globally.

**Indexer lag inflating hours_since:** Distinguish `_indextime` lag over two hours using `eval lag_mins=(_indextime-_time)/60`. Suppress paging with `where lag_mins<120` until catch-up finishes.
equipmentModels: Commvault Command Center REST API (backup job reports via HEC or scripted input)
Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601, backup job status and session data)
Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud (HEC for generic backup status and restore test ingest)
Splunk IT Service Intelligence (Splunkbase 1841, optional Glass Table for DR visualisation)
Veritas NetBackup OpsCenter REST API (job status via HEC or scripted input)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `backup` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `nis2_backup_schedule` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_backup_schedule')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(c)`. The control objective is: "Merges `backup:job_status` runs and `backup:restore_test` outcomes with `nis2_backup_schedule` so failed jobs, missed RPO windows, and failing drills share one timeline." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `backup` under `backup:job_status`, `backup:restore_test`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Install Splunk Add-on for Veeam (Splunkbase 5601) or send `backup:job_status` over HEC with extractions in `props.conf`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** Ingest restore-test reports as `backup:restore_test`. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 3:** Populate `nis2_backup_schedule.csv`. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Alert when `combined_risk=1`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** Archive weekly PDF with RFC3161 signing. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Backup Management and Disaster Recovery Verification," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIS2 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `nis2_backup_schedule` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`backup_policy, rpo_hours, rto_hours, last_successful_backup`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `system_name`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `nis2_backup_schedule` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval system_name = lower(system_name)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 14 days.

Numeric threshold `any_job_fail > 0`: this is the noise-to-signal decision point for the detection. Calibrate against your estate's baseline — see the 'Troubleshooting' section below for the calibration query.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.17: NIS2 Backup Management and Disaster Recovery Verification]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -14d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.17,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `any_job_fail`, `any_restore_fail`, `backup_policy`, `combined_risk`, `hours_since`, `job_status`, `lane`, `last_evt`, `rpo_breach`, `rpo_hours`. Run:

```spl
index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table any_job_fail, any_restore_fail, backup_policy, combined_risk, hours_since, job_status, lane, last_evt
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `nis2_backup_schedule`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup nis2_backup_schedule
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `any_job_fail`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(any_job_fail) as p50 perc90(any_job_fail) as p90 perc99(any_job_fail) as p99 max(any_job_fail) as hi
```

The current threshold `0` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.17: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Row-Level Table For Breach Reasons; Timeline Panel Of `Last Evt`; Single Value Widgets For Count Of Systems Past `Rpo Hours`; Optional Itsi Glass Table Linked By `System Key` (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We watch whether your nightly copies of important systems actually finish, and whether practice restores really worked. If a machine slips past the time window you promised, it shows up before you need it in a real emergency.
